_README: Editorial overrides for thinkatron.review. Keyed by interaction id (as string). Any field omitted falls back to the template placeholder. Fields: head (headline), stand (standfirst/dek), author_tags (array of author names shown in meta row). Grouped pieces go under _groups (see below) and are rendered as a single composite entry with Roman-numeral parts.
_groups_README: Each group renders as ONE entry on the site — a composite piece with Roman-numeral subsections. Keyed by a short group id (used as the URL slug: /entries/<id>.html). `ids` is the list of interaction ids in intended reading order (I, II, III…). Individual per-id overrides are ignored for ids inside a group; only group-level head/stand/author_tags apply. The composite entry's date = most recent part's timestamp.

temple.head: The temple and the performance
temple.stand: What if poems outlast their speakers not through mastery but through indifference to who is speaking? A diptych in which the corpus answers a question and then reverses the answer.

ballads.head: Ego and the ballads
ballads.stand: A three-part argument about what survives when the author dissolves — from Sweet William's Ghost through Love Gregor to Dryden's refusal of the whole premise.

11824.head: Sleve McDichael and the retrieval of poetry
11824.stand: A Japanese video game's fake baseball names become a test of what a poetry corpus can honestly return.

1115.head: The 4th Chamber and the compression of time
1115.stand: GZA, Ghostface, and RZA annex the past the way the canon only alludes to it — and Housman turns out to be the closest parallel.

929.head: Even the dead will not be safe
929.stand: Benjamin's theses on history meet Yeats's blade that stays razor-keen across five centuries. The past is not an archive — it is a battlefield.

1031.head: The Scholar-Gipsy and the stolen machine
1031.stand: Arnold's dropout who learns secret arts and never comes back is the patron saint of every coder who ran with the tools before anyone could charge for them.

1037.head: The pleasure of hating
1037.stand: Hazlitt insists that hatred is appetite, not failure — and the seventeenth century already knew this better than anyone since.

1071.head: I too, dislike it
1071.stand: Marianne Moore's famous contempt for poetry meets Barrett Browning's insistence that poetry must go where it is not wanted.

1127.head: When the apparatus collapses
1127.stand: Herbert, Tennyson, and King each build a poem about grief that breaks its own formal machinery — but the breaks are not all the same kind of break.

1218.head: The spectacle of the single man
1218.stand: Hazlitt on how admiration for the hero converts — his word — into contempt for everyone else. Shelley and Otway as evidence for the prosecution.

1221.head: The name of a thing
1221.stand: Marx on money-names and the severing of a thing from its history. The poets already knew — and Middleton's counterfeiters suggest the name might be all there is.

1223.head: The nailshop and the lyric
1223.stand: Geoffrey Hill's Mercian Hymns XXV short-circuits its own elegy. The refrain promises consolation; the stanza delivers inventory.

1242.head: The scaffold and the stage
1242.stand: A botched hanging in 1427 Paris becomes a test case for what happens when every institution fails except the condemned man himself.

1250.head: Before the law
1250.stand: Kafka's doorkeeper and Donne's law and Herbert's locked humours — three versions of the gate that was only ever meant for you.

11820.head: One of the four is not
11820.stand: R.F. Langley's Videlicet watches a bird that turns out not to be the bird it resembles — and breaks the English tradition of making birds into poets.
